title: Using a library of things
subtitle: Borrowing tools and equipment instead of buying them once
excerpt: A power drill is used for an average of 12 minutes in its lifetime. A library of things makes equipment like drills, tile cutters, camping gear, and party supplies available to borrow locally. The calculation of whether borrowing beats buying depends on frequency of use.
sourceNotes: Library of Things UK network data on membership and item categories. Sharing Cities Network guidance on tool libraries and lending libraries. WRAP consumption data on infrequently-used household items.

content.text: A library of things is a community organisation that lends physical items instead of books. Members pay an annual fee or a small borrowing charge and can access tools, equipment, and household items on loan for a few days at a time. The model avoids the environmental cost of manufacturing items that will be used rarely and the financial cost of buying them.
What libraries of things typically lend
Power tools: drills, jigsaws, sanders, tile cutters, pressure washers.
Garden equipment: lawn scarifiers, stump grinders, electric hedge trimmers, turf cutters.
Party and event equipment: folding tables, chairs, gazebos, urns, projectors.
Baby and child equipment: travel cots, bouncy chairs, highchairs, breast pumps.
Camping and outdoor gear: tents, sleeping bags, walking poles, cool boxes.
Household: steam cleaners, carpet cleaners, dehumidifiers, and kitchen gadgets used only once or twice a year.
When borrowing makes more sense than buying

The break-even point between borrowing and buying depends on how often the item will be used. An item needed once or twice a year is almost always better borrowed. An item needed weekly may be worth buying. The comparison should also factor in storage space: a seldom-used pressure washer takes up significant storage volume in a small house, with an ongoing cost of that space.
Finding a library of things near you

The Library of Things network (libraryofthings.co.uk) lists member libraries by postcode. Local tool libraries are also run by repair cafes, community centres, men's sheds, and some councils. Streetbank and OLIO (peer-to-peer sharing apps) connect residents who want to lend items informally. Some local councils run free tool lending schemes from libraries or council buildings.

The Library of Things network publishes an open-source handbook for starting a new library. The minimum viable version is a WhatsApp group among neighbours willing to lend specific items to each other. At this scale no insurance, storage, or administration is needed: members lend directly and arrange collection and return themselves.
